name: Nagakeshar
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Powerful Vata and Pitta Hara (pacifies Vata and Pitta doshas). Due to its Kashaya (astringent) and Tikta (bitter) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Raktastambhana' (hemostatic) and 'Amavata-hara' (rheumatic-reliever) therapies, clearing the fiery and agitated qualities of Pitta while balancing the dry and cold qualities of Vata through its systemic warming and astringent property.
Karma (Actions): Raktastambhana (hemostatic), Pachana (digestive), Varnya (complexion-promoting), Kandughna (anti-itch), Vishaghna (anti-toxic), and Jwarahara (antipyretic).

API/AFI Adherence: The dried stamen is the official medicinal part as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the central ingredient in iconic classical formulations like Nagakeshara Yoga, Eladi Ghrita, and various bleeding-control tonics.
Specific Indications: Used in Arsha (Piles), Raktapitta (Bleeding disorders), Shwasa (Asthma), Kasa (Cough), Visha (Toxic conditions), and Kushtha (Skin diseases).
usageForm: Stamen powder (Churna), Medicated oil (Taila), Decoction (Kwatha), Medicated ghee (Ghrita).
mainMedicinalUses: Expert Hemostatic Specialist (Raktastambhana): Nagakeshar is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all conditions of internal and external bleeding, particularly bleeding piles (Arsha). Its 'Raktastambhana' (hemostatic) and 'Kashaya' (astringent) nature provided by mesuanic acid and xanthones helps in providing instant hemostatic control and cooling the local inflammatory environment. WHO-aligned research confirms its role in managing excessive uterine and rectal bleeding.

Respiratory and Asthma Management: Historically known as 'Shwasahara' (antiasthmatic). It effectively reduces the intensity and frequency of asthma attacks by clearing 'Kapha-avarana' (mucus-obstruction) in the respiratory channels and providing systemic warming (Ushna).

Skin Health and Complexion: Known as 'Varnya' (beneficial for skin). Its aromatic and blood-purifying properties help in treating chronic skin conditions and improving facial radiance. The stamen paste applied topically promotes rapid relief from acne and sun-burns while providing systemic 'Varnya' (complexion-promoting) effects.

Antipyretic and Chronic Fever: Acts as a powerful agent for managing 'Jirna-jwara' (chronic fevers). It promotes systemic cleansing and clears 'Ama' (metabolic toxins) responsible for lingering body heat and aches.

Anti-inflammatory and Pain Relief: Systemically used for conditions like rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata) and chronic joint pain by clearing blocked Vata pathways and reducing local swelling through its hot-sharp property.

Wound Healing and Internal Damage: Traditionally used in 'Eladi Ghrita' to manage internal ulcers, bleeding, and lung lesions by promoting healthy tissue regeneration through its 'Sandhaniya' (healing) quality.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in Xanthones (Mesuaxanthone A, B), Coumarins (Mammeisin), Essential Oil (approx. 0.5-1.0%), and diversos Amino Acids. Mesuaxanthone is the primary bioactive responsible for its potent anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, and hemostatic action.
plantProfile: Unique History: A highly sacred and valued tree in India, often called the 'Nag-Keshar' or 'Cobras-Saffron'. Mentioned in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita for its unmatched 'Raktastambhana' (hemostatic) and 'Amavata-hara' (rheumatic-relieving) properties. It is spiritually associated with 'Sattva' (purity and clarity). Mentioned in classical Sanskrit literature as the 'Ironwood Tree'.

Physical Characteristics: A medium-to-large evergreen tree reaching up to 15-20 meters in height, with a majestic crown and brownish-grey, rough bark. The leaves are narrow, lanceolate, and glossy green with white-to-pink new growth. The flowers are large, solitary, and creamy-white with numerous golden-yellow stamens (Nagakeshar). The fruit is an oval-to-globose capsule. It is found throughout India, particularly in the Western Ghats and Northeast forests. It is widely cultivated for medicinal and ornamental use.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Primarily contains Xanthones (Mesuaxanthone), Coumarins (Mammesin), and Essential Oil. The presence of mesuaxanthone is crucial for its neuro-relaxant, anti-inflammatory, and anti-tubercular effects (when Appropriately diluted).

Strict Standardizations: The stamen is standardized in API based on its golden-yellow color and characteristic strong-sweet-aromatic odor. Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<6%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>15%), and water-soluble extractive (>10%). HPLC is used to monitor 1% to 2% xanthones in commercial extracts.
preparationMethods: Stamen Powder (Churna): 1-3g of fine powder taken twice daily with honey or warm water (for piles/bleeding) or warm milk (for general vitality).
Nagakeshara Yoga: Combined with Cardamom, Clove, and Ginger for systemic digestive and bleeding support.
Eladi Ghrita: 5-10g of ghee processed with Nagakeshar; taken to treat chronic respiratory Vata and physical weakness through its 'Brimhaniya' (nourishing) quality.
Nagakeshara Taila: Medicated oil used primarily for local application on the chest (respiratory) and joints (pain-relief).
External Paste: Ground fresh stamen or powder applied directly to the forehead for fever and headache or to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, skin rashes) to reduce heat and swelling.

dosages.Children: Generally safe. 125 mg to 250 mg of powder mixed with honey once or twice daily for digestive protection and cough relief.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 1-3 grams; Decoction: 20-50 ml; Ghee: 5-10 g; Taila: 1-2 drops (internal, with precautions), divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Ideal for age-related Vata issues and maintaining cardiac health. Dosage: 1g of powder daily with warm milk at bedtime.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Generally very safe. However, individuals with high Pitta (burning sensation, hyperacidity) should use it in moderation as it is Ushna (heating). Avoid during pregnancy or lactation in very large therapeutic doses except under professional guidance.

GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb and incense with a history of use spanning millennia across various global cultures and traditional systems (Ayurveda, Unani, and Himachali medicine) without documented toxicity.

Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside synthetic anti-hypertensive or anticoagulant medications as it may potentiate their effects. No severe adverse interactions reported in clinical literature.
Adverse Effects: None documented at traditional therapeutic levels. Excessive use in very sensitive individuals may rarely cause mild skin warmth or stomach dryness.
